*highlighted section*  
Yes, it is possible to use a different `create_app` function when running your Flask app as a server versus when running a CLI command. However, there are several ways to achieve this, and it depends on how you structure your application. One common approach is to use environment variables, which allows you to pass different configurations or parameters when running the CLI command. This method is clean and avoids hardcoding values into your application. But if you want to avoid setting environment variables, you can use a flag or a parameter that is passed explicitly when invoking the CLI command. This way, you can determine whether the app is being run as a server or as a CLI command without relying on external environment variables.

*highlighted section*  
In Flask, the `create_app` function is typically called when the application is initialized, and it returns a Flask application instance. When running the app as a server using `flask run`, the `create_app` function is used to create the app instance. However, when running a CLI command using `flask my_command`, the app instance is already created, and the `create_app` function is not called again. This means that you cannot directly use a different `create_app` function when running the CLI command. Instead, you can pass parameters to the CLI command and use those parameters to determine how the CLI command behaves.

To handle this, you can define a separate function that creates the app instance and passes it to the CLI command. This function can take parameters that are used to customize the behavior of the app. For example, you can define a `create_app` function that accepts a `config` parameter, which can be used to set different configurations for the app. This allows you to use different configurations when running the app as a server versus when running the CLI command. However, you cannot directly pass parameters to the `create_app` function when running the CLI command. Instead, you can use a flag or a parameter that is passed when invoking the CLI command.

If you want to avoid setting environment variables, you can use a flag or a parameter that is passed when invoking the CLI command. For example, you can define a CLI command that accepts a `--config` flag, which can be used to specify a different configuration file or a different set of parameters. This way, you can determine whether the app is being run as a server or as a CLI command without relying on environment variables.
